description: Domain knowledge, clinical decisions, medical expertise
_comment: IMPORTANT: These tests verify clinical knowledge and reasoning. The system should answer medical questions directly from its training knowledge. Do NOT expect tool calls for clinical questions - the system should demonstrate medical expertise through comprehensive, accurate answers.

cases.input: For a patient with anal cancer receiving concurrent chemoradiation with a brachytherapy boost, what are the typical treatment volume definitions and dose prescription? How does interstitial brachytherapy compare to endoluminal approaches?
cases.description: Medical knowledge test: anal cancer brachytherapy boost interstitial endoluminal

cases.input: Dose-rate effect in brachytherapy including biological effectiveness, repair half-time values, proraction effects, clinical evidence comparing LDR vs HDR, mathematical modeling approaches, and repair-misrepair model application.

Complete explanation of EQD2 as applied to brachytherapy including mathematical derivation, fractionation scheme conversion, cumulative dose combining for EBRT + brachytherapy, volume effects, and common pitfalls. Provide numerical examples.

BED mathematical framework, tissue-specific alpha/beta ratios, comparison of fractionation schedules, therapeutic ratio optimization, and specific calculation examples for common brachytherapy schedules.

Complete mechanism of cisplatin radiosensitization in cervical cancer including DNA damage mechanisms, synergy with radiation, optimal timing/dosing, GOG trial evidence, resistance mechanisms, and brachytherapy integration.

Dosimetric and radiobiological considerations for combining EBRT and brachytherapy: therapeutic window, dose gradient advantage, combined dose optimization, timing, OAR dose budgeting, and sequential delivery rationale.

Complete physics of inverse square law in brachytherapy: mathematical formulation, clinical implications for applicator selection, interaction with dwell time optimization, dose gradient index, and deviations from ideal behavior.

Clinical evidence and radiobiological rationale for hypofractionated prostate brachytherapy: low alpha/beta ratio, BED equivalence between LDR and HDR, clinical trial evidence, long-term outcomes, and patient selection.

Mechanism of applicator-related complications: organ perforation (incidence, risk factors, prevention), hemorrhage (vascular anatomy, hemostasis), infection (microbiology, prophylaxis), applicator migration, and nerve injury.
Dose heterogeneity in brachytherapy: inherent vs unplanned, TCP/NTCP impact, dose non-uniformity ratio, clinical significance of hot/cold spots, and management strategies.

Molecular biology of radiation-induced fibrosis: TGF-beta signaling, inflammatory cytokines, ROS mechanisms, cellular senescence, genetic susceptibility, temporal evolution, and anti-fibrotic therapeutic strategies.

Manchester system for intracavitary brachytherapy: point A/B definitions, biological significance, limitations of point-based dosimetry, applicator geometry effects, loading patterns, and evolution to volume-based dosimetry.

Pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic considerations for concurrent chemotherapy during brachytherapy: cell cycle effects, therapeutic ratio modification, drug mechanisms (cisplatin, 5-FU, MMC), timing, bone marrow recovery, wound healing, and trial evidence.

Complete physics of brachytherapy source calibration: RAKR definition, well chamber methodology, traceability chain (NIST/NPL), source decay characteristics (Ir-192, Co-60, Cs-137), verification procedures, and regulatory documentation.

Complete DVH parameter concepts: D90, D100, V100, D2cc definitions and rationale, dose homogeneity index, parameter relationship in plan optimization, and site-specific targets (cervical D90 >85 Gy, prostate V100 >95%).

Organ-at-risk dose constraints using bladder as example: serial vs parallel architecture, D2cc rationale, DVH parameters predicting toxicity, dose-volume correlation evidence from EMBRACE, EQD2 constraints for LDR/HDR, and EBRT contribution to OAR dose budgets.

Complete TPS commissioning for brachytherapy: acceptance testing, TG-59 tests, source data verification, dose calculation algorithm validation, source exchange commissioning, imaging protocol optimization, and ongoing QA.

Dose optimization in brachytherapy: forward vs inverse planning, optimization algorithms (gradient, simulated annealing, genetic), dwell time optimization, constrained vs unconstrained, objectives/constraints, clinical trade-offs, and ML in optimization.

Tandem and ovoid applicator system: historical development, anatomical basis, mechanical principles, dosimetric characteristics, applicator selection criteria, loading patterns (Manchester vs modern), and ring vs ovoid design comparison.

Image-guided adaptive brachytherapy for cervical cancer: adaptive definition, MRI protocol, target delineation on MRI, dose optimization per fraction, cumulative dose reporting methodology, and clinical outcomes vs conventional 2D.

Pulse-dose-rate brachytherapy: physical delivery method, radiobiological rationale, equivalence to LDR/HDR (incomplete repair model), clinical applications, technical advantages, dosimetric considerations, and clinical evidence.

Brachytherapy for head and neck cancer: historical role in oral cavity, interstitial technique, dose/fractionation, OAR relationships (mandible, salivary glands), functional outcomes, current role vs IMRT/TORS, and remaining indications.

DVH analysis in brachytherapy: cumulative vs differential DVH construction, statistical parameters, limitations as 2D representation, combined analysis with spatial maps, DSH for hollow organs, and GEC-ESTRO/EMBRACE reporting requirements.

Brachytherapy source security and transportation: NRC/state regulations, transportation procedures, storage requirements (vault design), chain of custody, source tracking systems, emergency procedures for loss/theft, training requirements, and violation penalties.

Applicator reconstruction in treatment planning: 2D point-based vs 3D CT vs MRI methods, imaging protocols, challenges (artifacts), software tools, verification procedures, impact on dose calculation, and QA procedures.

Tumor hypoxia and brachytherapy implications: mechanisms (perfusion vs diffusion-limited), OER value, oxygen fixation hypothesis, clinical evidence in gynecologic cancers, hypoxia assessment methods, strategies to overcome, and brachytherapy's dose escalation advantage.

Radiation-induced second malignancies after brachytherapy: risk estimates, latency periods, dose-response models (LNT vs threshold), site-specific risks, dose gradient advantage vs EBRT, chemotherapy additive risk, and surveillance recommendations.

Tumor control probability model in brachytherapy: Poisson formulation, parameter effects (cell density, radiosensitivity), dose heterogeneity impact, EUD concept, clinical validation (EMBRACE dose-response), limitations, and optimization application.

Normal tissue complication probability model in brachytherapy: LKB formulation, organ parameters (TD50, m, n), volume effect for serial/parallel organs, DVH reduction rationale, EMBRACE validation, limitations, and multi-criteria optimization application.

Plan quality metrics in brachytherapy: COIN definition/interpretation, homogeneity index, conformity index, coverage index, limitations of single metrics, clinical applications, and Pareto-optimal multi-objective evaluation.

Pediatric brachytherapy: unique considerations (growth, second malignancy), common tumor sites (vaginal RMS, ependymoma, retinoblastoma), applicator design challenges, developing organ dose constraints, anesthesia requirements, psychosocial impact, and long-term follow-up.

Radiation safety in brachytherapy: ALARA principle, time-distance-shielding, regulatory dose limits, HDR room design (interlocks, monitoring), permanent seed precautions, waste management, and emergency procedures (stuck source, contamination).

Quality of life impact of cervical cancer brachytherapy: validated instruments (EORTC QLQ-CX24, FACT-Cx), affected domains, acute/long-term outcomes, sexual function impact, technique comparison (2D vs IGABT), interventions (dilation, rehabilitation), and decision-making integration.

Brachytherapy for vulvar cancer: primary and adjuvant indications, applicator selection (interstitial, surface), target volume definition, dose/fractionation, OAR constraints (urethra, clitoris, femoral vessels), wound healing considerations, functional outcomes, and published evidence.

Brachytherapy for esophageal cancer: historical role, indications (definitive, palliative, neoadjuvant), applicator selection, dose/fractionation, OAR constraints (trachea, aorta, spinal cord), complications (fistula, perforation), trial evidence, and current role.

A 55-year-old woman with Stage IIB cervical cancer has completed 45 Gy of pelvic EBRT and is now being evaluated for brachytherapy boost. The MRI shows a residual tumor with parametrial extension. The brachytherapy team is debating between a tandem and ovoids versus a tandem and interstitial needle approach. Analyze the clinical decision-making factors that should guide this choice, including tumor response, anatomy, expected dose coverage, and toxicity risks.
cases.description: Applicator selection clinical decision for cervical cancer

cases.input: A 70-year-old man with intermediate-risk prostate cancer is deciding between HDR brachytherapy, LDR permanent seed implantation, and external beam radiation therapy. Present a comprehensive comparison of these three options including cure rates, side effect profiles, treatment convenience, and patient quality of life considerations to support his shared decision-making process.

A 45-year-old woman with a Stage II vaginal cancer is being evaluated for treatment. The tumor is located in the upper third of the vagina. The multidisciplinary team must decide between primary brachytherapy, EBRT alone, or a combination. Discuss the evidence supporting each approach, the factors that should influence the treatment decision, and how the decision should be documented in the multidisciplinary tumor board.

A patient with recurrent endometrial cancer at the vaginal cuff after hysterectomy is being considered for salvage brachytherapy. The recurrence is small (1.5 cm) and superficial. However, the patient also has severe cardiac disease limiting anesthesia options. Analyze the decision-making process for determining whether salvage brachytherapy is appropriate, what modifications might be needed, and how to counsel the patient about the risks and benefits.
cases.description: Salvage brachytherapy decision with cardiac limitations

cases.input: A center must decide whether to implement MRI-guided brachytherapy given limited resources. Present a structured decision analysis framework that considers clinical evidence, cost-effectiveness, patient outcomes, staffing requirements, and the impact on existing brachytherapy workflows. What decision criteria should be used, and how should the decision be communicated to stakeholders?

A center is evaluating whether to start offering brachytherapy for head and neck cancers. The nearest center offering this service is 300 miles away. Analyze the decision-making framework for establishing this service, including the required volume of cases to maintain competency, the infrastructure and staffing investments needed, and the expected impact on patient access and outcomes.
cases.description: Head and neck brachytherapy service establishment decision

cases.input: A 35-year-old woman with a small (2 cm) Stage IB1 cervical cancer is being counseled about her treatment options: radical hysterectomy with lymph node dissection, or definitive chemoradiation with brachytherapy. She is concerned about fertility preservation. Analyze the decision-making process considering oncological outcomes, fertility implications, treatment toxicity, and long-term quality of life.
cases.description: Young cervical cancer patient treatment decision

cases.input: A 78-year-old man with a T1a glottic laryngeal cancer is being considered for brachytherapy versus transoral laser microsurgery. Both options have excellent local control rates. Analyze the decision-making process comparing these two organ-preserving approaches, considering voice quality outcomes, treatment invasiveness, patient preferences, and the available evidence from comparative studies.
cases.description: Glottic cancer brachytherapy vs laser decision

cases.input: A multidisciplinary team is treating a 60-year-old woman with locally advanced vulvar cancer. The tumor is 4 cm and involves the clitoris and urethral meatus. Standard surgical treatment would require radical vulvectomy withurinary diversion. The team is considering neoadjuvant chemoradiation followed by conservative surgery, or primary chemoradiation with brachytherapy boost. Analyze the decision-making process for this complex case.

A 55-year-old woman with a recurrence of cervical cancer in the pelvic sidewall after prior chemoradiation is being considered for re-irradiation with brachytherapy. The previous EBRT dose was 50 Gy and the recurrence is in an area that previously received significant dose. Analyze the decision-making process for re-irradiation including cumulative dose assessment, normal tissue tolerance, expected outcomes, and risk of severe late toxicity.
cases.description: Re-irradiation decision for recurrent cervical cancer

cases.input: A 40-year-old man with penile cancer involving the glans and distal shaft is being evaluated for treatment. The options include partial penectomy, Mohs surgery with reconstruction, or brachytherapy. He is very concerned about organ preservation and sexual function. Analyze the clinical decision-making process for this patient, comparing the organ preservation approaches and their functional outcomes.

A center is deciding between two different brachytherapy treatment planning systems. Present a structured comparison framework that evaluates dose calculation accuracy, optimization capabilities, user interface, DICOM compatibility, vendor support, training requirements, and total cost of ownership. How should this comparison be presented to the hospital's technology evaluation committee?

A 68-year-old woman with recurrent rectal cancer after prior abdominoperineal resection is being considered for brachytherapy. The recurrence is in the presacral space and is deemed unresectable. Analyze the decision-making process for using brachytherapy in this palliative setting, including expected symptom control, survival benefit, quality of life impact, and alternatives to brachytherapy.
cases.description: Palliative brachytherapy decision for rectal recurrence

cases.input: A center's quality metrics reveal that brachytherapy treatment times have been increasing over the past year, leading to patient scheduling delays. Analyze the decision-making process to identify root causes, evaluate potential solutions (such as workflow optimization, additional equipment, or staff training), and prioritize interventions based on impact and feasibility.

A 72-year-old man with a T2N0M0 prostate cancer (Gleason 7, PSA 12) is evaluating brachytherapy options. He has significant lower urinary tract symptoms (IPSS score 18) and a prostate volume of 60 cc. Analyze whether he is a suitable candidate for brachytherapy considering his LUTS, prostate volume, and cancer risk group. What modifications to the standard approach might be needed?

A 48-year-old woman with recurrent cervical cancer after hysterectomy is being evaluated for pelvic exenteration versus definitive re-irradiation with brachytherapy. The surgical approach would be very morbid. Analyze the clinical decision-making process comparing these two radical treatment options, including expected outcomes, toxicity profiles, quality of life implications, and patient preference considerations.

A center is evaluating whether to offer outpatient HDR brachytherapy for cervical cancer. Currently, all treatments are performed as inpatient procedures. Analyze the decision-making process for transitioning to outpatient treatment, including patient selection criteria, safety requirements, cost implications, patient convenience, and the evidence supporting outpatient HDR brachytherapy.
cases.description: Inpatient to outpatient HDR transition decision

cases.input: A 30-year-old woman with a history of familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) and desmoid tumor of the mesentery requiring radiation is being considered for brachytherapy versus EBRT. The brachytherapy approach could potentially spare more bowel from radiation. Analyze the decision-making process considering her unique cancer predisposition, long-term radiation risks, and the importance of bowel preservation.
cases.description: Brachytherapy decision for FAP patient with desmoid tumor

cases.input: A 65-year-old man with prostate cancer is being evaluated for brachytherapy as monotherapy. His prostate volume is 35 cc, PSA is 8 ng/mL, Gleason score is 3+4=7, and clinical stage is T1c. He also has a large inguinal hernia repair mesh that is adjacent to the prostate. Analyze whether he is an appropriate candidate for brachytherapy, considering both his cancer characteristics and the anatomical challenge posed by the hernia mesh.

A multidisciplinary team is discussing a patient with locally advanced pancreatic cancer. The options include standard chemoradiation, brachytherapy boost after chemoradiation, or stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT). Analyze the evidence supporting each approach, the patient-specific factors that should guide the decision, and how the decision should be communicated to the patient and family.
cases.description: Locally advanced pancreatic cancer treatment decision

cases.input: A 70-year-old woman with recurrent endometrial cancer in the vaginal vault is being considered for pelvic exenteration versus palliative chemotherapy with or without brachytherapy. She has significant cardiac disease and limited functional status. Analyze the decision-making framework for this patient, weighing the curative intent of surgery against the lower morbidity of combined chemotherapy and brachytherapy, and discuss how to align treatment intensity with the patient goals of care and life expectancy.
cases.description: Recurrent endometrial cancer treatment decision with comorbidities

cases.input: I have a 50-year-old male with a 3 cm anal canal squamous cell carcinoma (Stage II, T2N0M0). He has HIV with CD4 count of 350 and is on antiretroviral therapy with undetectable viral load. Standard treatment is concurrent chemoradiation (Nigro protocol). Should I modify the standard radiation dose or fractionation given his HIV status? Should I consider brachytherapy boost? What are the radiation tolerance considerations in HIV-positive patients? Should I adjust the concurrent chemotherapy?
cases.description: Treatment decision for anal cancer in HIV-positive patient

cases.input: I am evaluating a 72-year-old male with prostate cancer (Gleason 3+4, Stage T2b, PSA 10.5) who is on warfarin for a mechanical mitral valve (INR target 2.5-3.5). He has well-controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.2%) and mild chronic kidney disease (GFR 52). He wants definitive treatment. Should I recommend LDR brachytherapy, EBRT, or watchful waiting? How should I manage his warfarin during brachytherapy? What INR threshold is safe for the procedure? Should I recommend bridging anticoagulation?
cases.description: Treatment decision for prostate cancer with mechanical valve and anticoagulation

cases.input: I am treating a 55-year-old female with a 4 cm vulvar cancer (squamous cell, Stage II, 8 mm depth of invasion, 2 mm margins after radical vulvectomy). She has bilateral inguinal lymph node dissection with 1 of 12 nodes positive on the left. She needs adjuvant therapy. Should I recommend adjuvant radiation to the vulvar bed and inguinal regions? What dose and fractionation should I use? What concurrent chemotherapy would be appropriate? Should I consider brachytherapy boost to the vulvar bed?
cases.description: Adjuvant therapy decision for vulvar cancer with positive inguinal node

cases.input: I have a 70-year-old male with prostate cancer (Gleason 3+4, Stage T2a, PSA 8.5) who has severe anxiety disorder requiring ongoing benzodiazepine therapy. He wants LDR brachytherapy but is very anxious about the procedure. How should I manage his anxiety during the procedure? Should I recommend general anesthesia versus local anesthesia? What modifications to the brachytherapy procedure are needed for anxious patients? Should I consider alternative treatment approaches?
cases.description: Management decision for anxious patient undergoing brachytherapy
